title: BlueBomb
requirements: A Wii mini — BlueBomb is the only exploit that works on this model
A Raspberry Pi Pico W or ESP32 board (BlueBomb Micro)
A USB flash drive, FAT32 formatted (Wii mini has no SD card slot — USB works the same way)
warnings: Every time you modify a Wii there is a !!small chance of an unrecoverable brick!!. Follow each step exactly.
!!Do NOT!! follow old video tutorials for Wii mini hacking — they often recommend outdated software that can cause bricks.
Make a NAND backup when the guide tells you to. Don't skip it — it's your only recovery if something goes wrong.
_note: BlueBomb exploits a Bluetooth pairing flaw and is the only exploit that works on Wii mini, since it has no SD slot, no Wi-Fi, and no GameCube ports. This guide uses BlueBomb Micro with a Raspberry Pi Pico W or ESP32 — no Linux PC or second Wii required, unlike the other BlueBomb methods on wii.hacks.guide.

steps.title: Before you start
steps.body:
This guide installs the **Homebrew Channel**, **BootMii**, and **Priiloader** on your Wii mini using **BlueBomb** — a Bluetooth exploit triggered from a Raspberry Pi Pico W or ESP32 board. It's the only exploit that works on Wii mini.

Wii mini has no SD card slot, so everything here uses a **USB flash drive** in its place — the console treats it the same way.

First, I'll check that your USB drive is FAT32.

steps.title: Put the installer files on your USB drive
steps.body:
Graphite downloads the **HackMii Installer**, the **Priiloader Installer**, and **Load Priiloader**, and unpacks them onto your USB drive. The HackMii Installer's `boot.elf` needs to sit at the root of the drive so the Wii mini can find it during the exploit.

Just press the button and wait.

steps.title: Clean up and eject the drive
steps.body:
macOS scatters hidden junk files (`.DS_Store`, `._` files) onto USB drives, and Wii homebrew can choke on them.

Press the button, and Graphite strips those files off the drive and **safely ejects it for you**. Once it pops off, pull it out.

steps.title: Move the USB drive to your Wii mini
steps.body:
Take the USB drive out of your computer and plug it into the **USB port on the back of the Wii mini**.

Power the console on and go to **Wii Options** → **Wii Settings** to note the **system menu version letter** in the top-right corner — you'll need it (along with your region) to grab the right firmware for your Pico or ESP32.

Power the console back off once you have it. Press **I did this** to continue.

steps.title: Flash your Pico W or ESP32
steps.body:
**Raspberry Pi Pico W** (easiest):

1. Download the pre-compiled binaries from the [bluebomb_micro releases page](https://github.com/GaryOderNichts/bluebomb_micro/releases).
2. Hold the **BOOTSEL** button on the Pico while plugging it into your computer via USB.
3. Copy the `.uf2` file matching your console's region and system menu version (for example `bluebomb_WII_SM4_3E.uf2` for a European Wii mini on 4.3) onto the Pico drive that appears.
4. The Pico's LED turns solid once it's ready.

**ESP32** works too, but needs building from source — see the [ESP32 build instructions](https://github.com/GaryOderNichts/bluebomb_micro/tree/main/ports/esp32#building-from-source) if you'd rather use one of those.

A classic method also exists using a Linux PC's built-in Bluetooth, or a second Wii running BlueMii — see wii.hacks.guide/bluebomb if a microcontroller isn't an option for you.

steps.title: Trigger the exploit
steps.body:
Plug the Pico (or ESP32) into your computer's USB port for power.

Power on the Wii mini with **no Wii Remotes synced or nearby**. Keep the console within about 3 feet of the microcontroller.

Press the **Sync button** on the front of the console repeatedly until the Pico's LED starts flashing rapidly — that means it accepted the connection. The **HackMii Installer** should load on screen within a few seconds.

If nothing happens after a minute, power-cycle the console and try again.

steps.title: Install the Homebrew Channel
steps.body:
You're now in the **HackMii Installer**. The first screen is the scam warning — just a reminder not to pay for free software.

1. Wait 30 seconds for **Press 1 to continue** to appear, then press **1** on your Wii Remote.
2. You'll see the system test results. Select **Continue**.
3. Select **Install the Homebrew Channel**. It installs in a few seconds.
4. When the green tick appears, select **Continue**.

steps.title: Install BootMii
steps.body:
Still in the HackMii Installer, select **BootMii...**.

Select **Install BootMii as IOS**. Wait for the progress bars to complete. When the green tick appears, select **Continue**.

!!If you see the option to Install BootMii as boot2!! — this gives stronger brick protection. Install that too. (Most Wiis won't show this option; yours was made before 2009 if it does.)

Finally, select **Return to the main menu**, then **Exit**. The console boots into the **Homebrew Channel**.

steps.title: Launch BootMii
steps.body:
You're now at the **Homebrew Channel** — a grid of homebrew apps. Before installing Priiloader, make a NAND backup so you can recover from any future problems.

Press the **HOME** button on your Wii Remote, then select **Launch BootMii**.

(If the button doesn't appear, BootMii wasn't installed — re-run the exploit and install it again.)

You should see BootMii's main menu (the second picture). Use the **console's POWER/RESET buttons** (POWER = move right, RESET = select) — Wii mini has no GameCube ports.

steps.title: Make a NAND backup
steps.body:
This is !!your safety net!!. If anything ever goes wrong, this backup can bring the Wii back.

In BootMii, select the **Options** button (the gears icon). Then select the first icon on the left (NAND Backup).

BootMii dumps your NAND and shows the progress. "Bad Blocks" are normal — they come from the factory. When the dump finishes, BootMii verifies the backup. Ideally all blocks are green.

Press the **Back** button (arrow icon), then select either the **Wii Menu** or **Homebrew Channel** to exit BootMii.

Power off the Wii.

troubleshoot.label: The backup failed or has errors

steps.title: Save your NAND backup
steps.body:
Take the USB drive out of the Wii mini and plug it back into this computer.

Press the button and Graphite grabs the backup the console just made (`nand.bin` and `keys.bin`), copies it into a folder on your **Desktop**, and clears the files off the drive to free up space. If a backup folder already exists, it saves a new one alongside it.

This is optional; you can skip ahead with **Next**, but keeping the backup is strongly recommended.

steps.title: Install Priiloader
steps.body:
Priiloader is your brick protection — it loads before the System Menu, so even if the menu is corrupt, you can recover. Put the USB drive back in the Wii mini and power it on.

1. Launch the **Homebrew Channel**.
2. Open **Priiloader Installer** from the list.
3. Press the **+ Button** on your Wii Remote.
4. Wait for the install to complete, then press **A** to return to the Homebrew Channel.
5. Press **HOME**, then select **Exit to System Menu**. The console will load Priiloader's menu automatically.

steps.title: Configure Priiloader
steps.body:
Now enable the system menu hacks that keep your Wii safe:

1. In Priiloader, scroll down to **System Menu Hacks** and press **A**.
2. Enable the following:
   - **Block Disc Updates** (stops game discs from forcing updates)
   - **Block Online Updates** (stops Nintendo from force-updating you)
3. Scroll to **save settings** and press **A**.
4. Press **B** to go back, then select **System Menu**.

From now on, hold **RESET** while powering on to enter Priiloader. You're protected.

steps.title: You're done
steps.body:
Your Wii mini has the **Homebrew Channel**, **BootMii**, and **Priiloader** installed. You're fully protected and ready to run homebrew apps.

Drop homebrew apps into the `apps` folder on your USB drive and launch them from the Homebrew Channel. For USB loaders, cIOS, or restoring online services, check the guides at `wii.hacks.guide`.

To enter Priiloader in the future: hold **RESET** while powering on. To restore your NAND: launch BootMii from the Homebrew Channel or use the BootMii recovery guide.
